issue_title: az acs kubernetes get-credentials needs appended 'mgmt' to DNS prefix
issue_body:
When using the command from [the Kubernetes tutorial](https://docs.microsoft.com/de-de/azure/container-service/container-service-kubernetes-walkthrough) to get credentials, it should be mentioned that the DNS prefix you specify when creating the container service and the one you need to specify when doing

`az acs kubernetes get-credentials --dns-prefix=<...>`

are not the same; it seems you must append `mgmt` to the latter. E.g., I create a Kubernetes Cluster with DNS prefix `mykube`, then I need to get the credentials using `--dns-prefix=mykubemgmt`, which is rather confusing.

**Important**: I created my Kubernetes cluster using the Portal directly, not via the `az` command line tooling (including creating my own Service Principal and such). This also means that my `acs` name was prefixed with `containerservice-<what I wrote>`.

The error message you get otherwise is just this (here on macOS Sierra):

```bash
[Errno 8] nodename nor servname provided, or not known
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/myuser/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/azure/cli/main.py", line 34, in main
    cmd_result = APPLICATION.execute(args)
  File "/Users/myuser/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/azure/cli/core/application.py", line 139, in execute
    result = expanded_arg.func(params)
  File "/Users/myuser/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/azure/cli/core/commands/__init__.py", line 275, in _execute_command
    result = op(client, **kwargs) if client else op(**kwargs)
  File "/Users/myuser/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/azure/cli/command_modules/acs/custom.py", line 477, in k8s_get_credentials
    '.kube/config', path_candidate)
  File "/Users/myuser/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/azure/cli/command_modules/acs/acs_client.py", line 21, in SecureCopy
    ssh.connect(host, username=user, key_filename=os.path.join(home, '.ssh', 'id_rsa'))
  File "/Users/myuser/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/paramiko/client.py", line 296, in connect
    to_try = list(self._families_and_addresses(hostname, port))
  File "/Users/myuser/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/paramiko/client.py", line 200, in _families_and_addresses
    addrinfos = socket.getaddrinfo(hostname, port, socket.AF_UNSPEC, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
gaierror: [Errno 8] nodename nor servname provided, or not known
```

I also tried the client in docker (which uses Python 3.5); there the error message looks like this:

```bash
[Errno -2] Name does not resolve
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/azure/cli/main.py", line 34, in main
    cmd_result = APPLICATION.execute(args)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/azure/cli/core/application.py", line 142, in execute
    result = expanded_arg.func(params)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/azure/cli/core/commands/__init__.py", line 275, in _execute_command
    result = op(client, **kwargs) if client else op(**kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/azure/cli/command_modules/acs/custom.py", line 511, in k8s_get_credentials
    '.kube/config', path_candidate)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/azure/cli/command_modules/acs/acs_client.py", line 21, in SecureCopy
    ssh.connect(host, username=user, key_filename=os.path.join(home, '.ssh', 'id_rsa'))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/paramiko/client.py", line 296, in connect
    to_try = list(self._families_and_addresses(hostname, port))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/paramiko/client.py", line 200, in _families_and_addresses
    addrinfos = socket.getaddrinfo(hostname, port, socket.AF_UNSPEC, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/socket.py", line 732, in getaddrinfo
    for res in _socket.getaddrinfo(host, port, family, type, proto, flags):
socket.gaierror: [Errno -2] Name does not resolve
```

So either this is a documentation thing, or the behavior of the `az` cli tool has changed, or both. Right now, I'd say it's confusing.

**Update**: It works (with the changes I made in a PR) if you create the cluster using the `az` command line; so it seems the Container Services UI in the portal uses a different template than the `az` command line does.

issue_title: [App Service] Simplifying the local Git deployment configuration
issue_body:
After running the `az appservice web source-control config-local-git` command, I was expecting it to have created a new Git remote for my project, as opposed to requiring me to add it manually. Is this behavior available somewhere in the CLI? It's a simple gesture, but I feel like it would help simplify the getting started process for new users.

Additionally, is it obvious to users that they need to set a deployment password in order to actually push to the newly provisioned Git repository? After running `az appservice web source-control config-local-git`, it prints out the URL for the repo, which includes the user name, but if you immediately tried to push to it, you would be prompted for a password.

I wonder whether this command should either provide a hint to go run `az appservice web deployment user set`, or better yet, if it prompted you for the password automatically as part of the config step. 

The steps needed to setup a custom Git or Docker deployment seem simpler than using the local Git solution, and I wonder whether the CLI couldn't help smooth that over a little bit.

issue_title: Race condition due to `transfer` future `done_callback` that depends on state that may set after it's execution
issue_body:
### Description
I noticed this while trying to figure out why `client.run()` hangs in tests if the transfer function doesn't sleep a bit.

#### Background
Upon calling `submit` on a `ThreadPoolExecutor` a future is returned, but the task can be immediately executed (i.e. there's no `start()` on the pool).
A done callback can be a added on the future, but it depends on actually submitting the future.
`add_done_callback` has internal logic that will execute the callback immediately if the future has already finished (https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/concurrent/futures/_base.py#L376)

#### Actual problem
The problem is that the callback for transfer (`_update`) is dependent on state that is added only after it executes.
Mainly it checks for the future reference in `cfutures`

    if future in self._cfutures:
    ...

However the future is added to `cfutures` after submit and callback

```
    def _start(self, src, dst):
            ...
            future = self._submit(
                self._transfer, self._adlfs, src, name, offset,
                self._chunks[obj]['expected'], self._buffersize,
                self._blocksize)
            # THE CODE ABOVE DEPENDS ON THE FOLLOWING STATEMENT
            self._cfutures[future] = obj

...
    def _submit(self, fn, *args, **kwargs):
        kwargs['shutdown_event'] = self._shutdown_event
        future = self._pool.submit(fn, *args, **kwargs)        
        future.add_done_callback(self._update)
        return future
```

Later, `monitor` will wait until `self.active` returns `False`, which never happens since the condition `not self._fstates.contains_none('pending', 'transferring', 'merging')` is dependent on the branch that never gets executed in `_update`

---

### Reproduction Steps
Remove the `sleep` in one of the tests that calls `client.run()` (e.g. https://github.com/Azure/azure-data-lake-store-python/blob/master/tests/test_transfer.py#L36) and see how the test hangs.

### Environment summary

**SDK Version:** What version of the SDK  are you using? (pip show azure-datalake-store)
`0.0.14`

issue_title: Add "selective overwrite based on newest date modified" option for downloads and uploads
issue_body: This option would be useful in the case that, for example, I download some subdirectory of files, use some of them as inputs to calculations, and then re-upload the entire subdirectory with additional calculation outputs scattered within subfolders in the subdirectory. In the meantime however, someone has updated a new version of a file that I am using as an input. When I re-upload the subdirectory including outputs to the data lake, then my older, not updated version of the input file will overwrite the newer version uploaded by a colleague in the meantime. In this case, it would be good to have a selective overwrite based on only overwriting if the files are newer.

issue_title: Add a class to wrap TLS authentication
issue_body: [`prestodb.auth`](https://github.com/prestodb/presto-python-client/blob/master/prestodb/auth.py) provides an interface to setup authentication in the client. It currently defines the `Authentication` abstract interface and `KerberosAuthentication`. The purpose of this issue is to add a class to setup TLS client authentication.
We don’t skip sources marked as such, but we should.

issue_title: Support tags as well as shas
issue_body:
They kind-of work as-is unless tags collide across different hooks.

The simplest solution is to make the repository symlinks `repo + ref` instead of just `ref` as they are right now.

Then anything that git understands as `git checkout {0}` will work.

issue_title: Multiple Visuals in Link only use single material
issue_body:
Not sure if this is expected behavior. Ideally each visual is rendered with the material tagged within.

Right now I'm getting around this with a hack: making the second visual an entire different link (with fixed joint). In other apps the visuals render differently, so I think this is a pyBullet issue.  

In the below example, the entire bot is rendered red. However the body is blue.

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<robot name="bot">
  <material name="blue">
    <color rgba="0 0 0.8 1"/>
  </material>

   <material name="red">
    <color rgba="0.8 0 0 1"/>
  </material>

  <link name="body">
    <visual>
      <geometry>
        <box size="0.1016 0.0254 0.6604"/>
      </geometry>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0.3302" />
      <material name="blue"/>
    </visual>

    <visual>
      <geometry>
        <cylinder length="0.17" radius=".01"/>
      </geometry>
      <origin rpy="0 1.57 0" xyz="0 0 0.01905"/>
      <material name="red"/>
    </visual>
  </link>
</robot>
```

Just in case here is the tiny script to run:

```python
import pybullet as p
import time
import pybullet_data

physicsClient = p.connect(p.GUI)
p.setGravity(0,0,0)
planeId = p.loadURDF("simple.urdf")

for i in range (10000):
    p.stepSimulation()
    time.sleep(1./240.)

p.disconnect()
```
Here is the all red bot:

![red bot](https://i.imgur.com/AbZQHSN.png)

issue_title: [enhancement] Add ability to disable automatic decompression of body
issue_body:
In our library we'd like the ability to disable the automatic decompression of the request body as we need to compare a hash in the headers to the original body.

Right now this doesn't seem to be possible as the HTTP parser creates a DeflateBuffer which will automatically decompress the body.  If guidance is given how this should be achieved I can create a PR and test case.

issue_title: UX Bug when uploading multiple large files
issue_body:
Hello!  I noticed a bug when attempting to upload more than 1 large file using the Jupyter UI.

Steps to reproduce:

1. Open Jupyter home page (/tree)
2. Click Upload
3. In the browser's file upload dialog box, select two large files simultaneously, and then click OK/Open.
4. Jupyter will ask to confirm "Large file size warning" twice, once for each file, click OK to each.
5. Observe that two new entries are displayed in the list, with Upload/Cancel buttons next to them, but both entries are for the FIRST file.  (The second file is not listed.)

Expected:

5. Both files should be displayed in the tree with Upload/Cancel buttons.  There should be no duplication.

Thank you!

issue_title: Support animation as an extension to the interact function?
Allow code to be executed in markdown blocks and insert result
issue_body:
I was thinking that for researchers who are doing analysis from within jupyter it would be really nice if the results from some computation could be automatically pasted into the textual discussion of the results. Assuming the delimiter \& is used 

**Example**
Consider the following function: 

```
from math import sin, pi
def f(x): return sin(x)
```

Let us now compute \&`f(pi)`\& `=> 0`

Here we've actually called a function from within a markdown block and inserted its result in place of the function call.

issue_title: Selecting downwards to markdown cell is broken
issue_body:
1. Create a new notebook.
2. Make a bunch of cells, and convert them to markdown mode.
3. Select a cell other than the last cell, and ensure you are in command mode.
4. Press shift-down to extend the selection downwards.

Expected result: the cell selected in step 3 and the cell immediately below that are highlighted.

Actual result: the cursor moves down (as if I wasn't holding shift).
- Replicated on master with Firefox and Chromium
- It seems to be the visual highlighting going wrong; changing the cell type still affects all the cells that should be selected.
- The deciding thing seems to be if the cell the selection is extending _to_ is a markdown cell: I can select down from a markdown cell to a code cell, but not down from a code cell to a markdown cell.
- There is another bug when a range has been selected going up from the bottom, and then you press up or down without shift. I hope they're related and will both be fixed at once, but if not, I'll report that in more detail.

    rule = gen_rule_payload("from:jack",

I've found it useful to treat editor fields in the same way as author fields i.e. by writing a function that's the equivalent of `author()` that splits then into lists of 'Name, Surname':

```
def editor(record):
    """
    Split editor field into a list of "Name, Surname".
    :param record: the record.
    :type record: dict
    :returns: dict -- the modified record.
    """
    if "editor" in record:
        if record["editor"]:
            record["editor"] = getnames([i.strip() for i in record["editor"].replace('\n', ' ').split(" and ")])
        else:
            del record["editor"]
    return record
```

Do you think that this is worth adding to the main package?

iWorkflow / BIG-IQ (?) support REST proxy URI. This makes it possible to apply tenant RBAC on the REST endpoints (i.e. limit a user to only be able to modify a single BIG-IP LTM pool in a single Partition).

Documented here: https://devcentral.f5.com/wiki/iControl.REST_proxy_APIs.ashx

Example of one of the changes required (this is not meant to be a "good" example, but it gets the job done). This was done to monkey patch the library:
```

class ManagementRootProxy(ManagementRoot):
    """An interface to a multiple BIG-IP"""
    def __init__(self, hostname, username, password, device_uuid, **kwargs):
        timeout = kwargs.pop('timeout', 30)
        port = kwargs.pop('port', 443)
        icontrol_version = kwargs.pop('icontrol_version', '')
        if kwargs:
            raise TypeError('Unexpected **kwargs: %r' % kwargs)
        # _meta_data variable values
        iCRS = iControlRESTSession(username, password, timeout=timeout)
        # define _meta_data
        self._meta_data = {
            'allowed_lazy_attributes': [Tm, Cm, Shared],
            'hostname': hostname,
            'port': port,
            'uri': 'https://%s:%s/mgmt/shared/resolver/device-groups/cm-cloud-managed-devices/devices/%s/rest-proxy/mgmt/' % (hostname, port,device_uuid),
            'icr_session': iCRS,
            'device_name': None,
            'local_ip': None,
            'bigip': self,
            'icontrol_version': icontrol_version,
            'username': username,
            'password': password,
            'tmos_version': None,
        }
        self._get_tmos_version()

```
This needs to be added to the SDK to fullfill the Ansible PR https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-ansible/issues/621

# Introducing .NET IL Linker

The .NET Core team has been working on a linker in collaboration with the Mono team. It is based on the [Mono Linker](https://github.com/mono/linker) project, and by extension, on the [Cecil](https://github.com/jbevain/cecil) project.

The goal of the .NET IL Linker project is two part:

* Provide a capable linker for .NET Core to make it easy to significantly reduce .NET Core application sizes.
* Establish the Mono Linker as the primary linker for the .NET ecosystem.

You can start using the linker today, using the following instructions:

* [Using the .NET IL Linker](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/samples/linker-instructions.md)
* [Using IL Linker Advanced Features](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/samples/linker-instructions-advanced.md)

## Details

In trivial cases, the linker can reduce the size of applications by 50%. The size wins may be more favorable or more moderate for larger applications. The linker removes code in your application and dependent libraries that are not reached by any code paths. It is effectively an application-specific dead code analysis.

The IL Linker currently only supports [.NET Core Self Contained Applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/deploying/) with this first preview version. You can try using the linker with an example [self-contained .NET Core application sample](https://github.com/dotnet/dotnet-docker-samples/blob/master/dotnetapp-selfcontained/README.md). The primary difference with this application is that it has a reference to the [ILLink.Tasks package](https://dotnet.myget.org/feed/dotnet-core/package/nuget/Illink.Tasks) in its [cspoj project file](https://github.com/dotnet/dotnet-docker-samples/blob/master/dotnetapp-selfcontained/dotnetapp.csproj).

The Linker provides an option to print a size improvement chart that describes binaries that have been removed from an application or that have been reduced in size. It also provides the total % size reduction for the application. You can see an example of one of these charts in the following example.

![.NET IL Linker Size Improvement Chart](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2608468/29877374-4d72ae62-8d54-11e7-9da3-3cc22f58f4f9.png)

Please provide feedback on your use of the linker. We are actively looking for feedback to improve the linker. In particular, we are looking for feedback on the following topics:

* Linker throughput.
* Cases where the linker can be more aggressive.
* Cases where the link is too aggressive and causes applications to fail at runtime.
* The linker provided an excellent result for a large application.

Please create issue with your feedback at either [mono/linker](https://github.com/mono/linker/issues) or [dotnet/core](https://github.com/dotnet/core/issues). Also feel free to contact the team at clrlinker@microsoft.com.

And, it wouldn't be right to announce this linker without a hat tip to [Joel Spolsky](https://www.joelonsoftware.com) who we can give the honorary and historical distinction of [feature requestor](https://www.joelonsoftware.com/2004/01/28/please-sir-may-i-have-a-linker/).

issue_title: Easier creation of RSACryptoServiceProvider from public key data
issue_body:
# Issue Title

Currently when using public/private signing/encrypting using RSA, it's not possible to directly create an instance of RSACryptoServiceProvider easily, given just a public key in PEM (or variant) format.

# General

Given a PEM, in order to create an instance of RSACryptoServiceProvider it requires the programmer to have a knowledge of ASN.1, know that a PEM is in this format and how to correctly specify the AsnEncodedData settings accordingly after parsing and base64 decoding a PEM - a high bar for entry when trying to use it. This appears on all versions of .NET and OSes to date that I'm aware of.

Example of this issue occurring; [Correctly Create RSACryptoServiceProvider from public key
](https://stackoverflow.com/q/41808094/1838819)
Given the relatively recent post and the specificity of the issue, the view count is higher than would be expected - people are encountering/searching this regularly.

A proposed way forward would be for a constructor (or similarly accessible route) to create the necessary RSACryptoServiceProvider directly from given PEM data (bytes, file, base64 data.... [).](http://lapo.it/asn1js)

The current version on github has all the changes currently scheduled for the 2.6.0 release. Like 2.5.0, this another big one - mostly because of the time it took to get the ambiguous datetime stuff right. Here is the new NEWS file (which should ping those who have made contributions):

- Added PEP-495-compatible methods to address ambiguous and imaginary dates in
  time zones in a backwards-compatible way. Ambiguous dates and times can now
  be safely represented by all dateutil time zones. Many thanks to Alexander
  Belopolski (@abalkin) and Tim Peters @tim-one for their inputs on how to
  address this. Original issues reported by Yupeng and @zed (lP: 1390262,
  gh issues #57, #112, #249, #284, #286, prs #127, #225, #248, #264, #302).
- Added new methods for working with ambiguous and imaginary dates to the tz
  module. datetime_ambiguous() determines if a datetime is ambiguous for a given
  zone and datetime_exists() determines if a datetime exists in a given zone.
  This works for all fold-aware datetimes, not just those provided by dateutil.
  (gh issue #253, gh pr #302)
- Fixed an issue where dst() in Portugal in 1996 was returning the wrong value
  in tz.tzfile objects. Reported by @abalkin (gh issue #128, pr #225)
- Fixed an issue where zoneinfo.ZoneInfoFile errors were not being properly
  deep-copied. (gh issue #226, pr #225)
- Refactored tzwin and tzrange as a subclass of a common class, tzrangebase, as
  there was substantial overlapping functionality. As part of this change,
  tzrange and tzstr now expose a transitions() function, which returns the
  DST on and off transitions for a given year. (gh issue #260, pr #302)
- Deprecated zoneinfo.gettz() due to confusion with tz.gettz(), in favor of
  get() method of zoneinfo.ZoneInfoFile objects. (gh issue #11, pr #310)
- For non-character, non-stream arguments, parser.parse now raises TypeError
  instead of AttributeError. (gh issues #171, #269, pr #247)
- Fixed an issue where tzfile objects were not properly handling dst() and
  tzname() when attached to datetime.time objects. Reported by @ovacephaloid.
  (gh issue #292, pr #309)
- /usr/share/lib/zoneinfo was added to TZPATHS for compatibility with Solaris
  systems. Reported by @dhduvall (gh issue #276, pr #307)
- tzoffset and tzrange objects now accept either a number of seconds or a
  datetime.timedelta() object wherever previously only a number of seconds was
  allowed. (gh pr #264, #277)
- datetime.timedelta objects can now be added to relativedelta objects. Reported
  and added by Alec Nikolas Reiter (@justanr) (gh issue #282, pr #283
- Refactored relativedelta.weekday and rrule.weekday into a common base class
  to reduce code duplication. (gh issue #140, pr #311)
- An issue where the WKST parameter was improperly rendering in str(rrule) was
  reported and fixed by Daniel LePage (@dplepage). (gh issue #262, pr #263)
- A replace() method has been added to rrule objects by @jendas1, which creates
  new rrule with modified attributes, analogous to datetime.replace (gh pr #167)
- Made some significant performance improvements to rrule objects in Python 2.x
  (gh pr #245)
- All classes defining equality functions now return NotImplemented when
  compared to unsupported classes, rather than raising TypeError, to allow other
  classes to provide fallback support. (gh pr #236)
- Several classes have been marked as explicitly unhashable to maintain
  identical behavior between Python 2 and 3. Submitted by Roy Williams 
  (@rowillia) (gh pr #296)
- Trailing whitespace in easter.py has been removed. Submitted by @OmgImAlexis
  (gh pr #299)
- Windows-only batch files in build scripts had line endings switched to CRLF.
  (gh pr #237)
- @adamchainz updated the documentation links to reflect that the canonical
  location for readthedocs links is now at .io, not .org. (gh pr #272)
- Made some changes to the CI and codecov to test against newer versions of
  Python and pypy, and to adjust the code coverage requirements. For the moment,
  full pypy3 compatibility is not supported until a new release is available,
  due to upstream bugs in the old version affecting PEP-495 support.
  (gh prs #265, #266, #304, #308)
- The full PGP signing key fingerprint was added to the README.md in favor of
  the previously used long-id. Reported by @valholl (gh issue #287, pr #304)
- Updated zoneinfo to 2016i. (gh issue #298, gh pr #306)

If anyone has any objections or last-minute corrections, please let me know. I'll note that I haven't been too concerned with the documentation yet, but that should be coming relatively soon. I think that there will also be some additional behind-the-scenes improvements to the way `fromutc()` is handled for most of the `tz` classes provided here, but for now they should all work.

(Ping @jarondl)

If an `rrule` is created with timezone information in `DTSTART`, it appears to function correctly, but converting it back to a `str` removes the timezone information from `DTSTART`.

```python
>>> from dateutil.rrule import rrulestr
>>> str(rrulestr('DTSTART:20180102T120000Z\nFREQ=DAILY'))
'DTSTART:20180102T120000\nFREQ=DAILY'
```

Expected output:
`DTSTART:20180102T120000Z\nFREQ=DAILY`

OS: Alpine Linux 3.7
dateutil version 2.6.1
Python 3.6.3

I am using RRULE strings to store date/time information in a database. For now, I'm working around this by managing `DTSTART` separately from the RRULE strings and stripping it when serializing/deserializing the RRULE.

This is a companion issue to #449, since `relativedelta` operations can, I believe, be decomposed into two operations:

1. Replace all specified date components with the components from a partial datetime
2. Add or subtract a "calendar" delta

The documentation documents the application of the absolute and relative arguments as interleaved (absolute year first, then offset by years), but I think that because it starts from the largest argument and moves to the smallest, the operations can be decomposed as above, since the addition or subtraction of a larger component should never affect a smaller component.

Once both components are refactored out of `relativedelta`, `relativedelta` can simply encapsulate the two for as long as we want to maintain the `relativedelta` interface.

Right now we're just using the trove classifiers, so I think people may end up doing some complicated pinning, which is less than ideal (see #536 and #526). What we need is the `Requires-Python:` metadata in pip. I think this is added through `setup.py`. Here are some examples of people using it:

- [pytest](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/master/setup.py#L86)
- [ipython](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/master/setup.py#L235)

@carreau gave [this talk at PyBay](https://youtu.be/3i6n1RwqQCo?t=660) (link goes to the relevant time - 11 minutes in) where he discusses the `python_requires` option ([slides are here](https://speakerdeck.com/pybay/2017-building-bridges-stopping-python-2-without-damages)), probably best to listen to what he's talking about and avoid or document the pitfalls here.

issue_title: tzwin for the same timezone string does not compare equal
issue_body:
Consider this:

```
>>> dateutil.tz.gettz('Eastern Standard Time') == dateutil.tz.gettz('Eastern Standard Time')
False
```

Other timezone classes such as tzfile or tzlocal compare equal:

```
>>> dateutil.tz.gettz('America/New_York') == dateutil.tz.gettz('America/New_York')
True
>>> dateutil.tz.gettz('America/New_York') == dateutil.tz.gettz('America/Halifax')
False
>>> dateutil.tz.tzlocal() == dateutil.tz.tzlocal()
True
```

Note that, as a consequence of this, the expression ```dateutil.tz.gettz('UTC') == dateutil.tz.gettz('UTC')``` will return different results on different platforms (False on Windows, True on Unices).

issue_title: Using geopandas DataFrames in Choropleth Maps
issue_body:
In passing, I noted that it's possible use geopandas in the context of `.choropleth()` by calling something like:

```
m.choropleth(
    #geodata is a geopandas geodataframe
    geo_str=geodata.to_json(),
    ...
)
```

which may feel more natural to some users.

If this is a widely used way of calling `.choropleth()`, adding a `geo_df` attribute to allow `geo_df = geodata` could make sense, but if it's an edge case it runs the risk of overcomplicating the interface to `.choropleth()` unnecessarily? 

One advantage of using `geopandas` dataframes is that it makes it easy to merge data from several geojson files that share a similar model.

```
import geopandas as gpd
import pandas as pd

#Not sure if there's another way of merging geopandas geodataframes?
#via http://gis.stackexchange.com/a/162661
geodata = gpd.GeoDataFrame( pd.concat( [gpd.read_file(f) for f in geojsonFiles], ignore_index=True) )

centre_lat,centre_lon=((geodata.bounds['minx'].min()+geodata.bounds['maxx'].max())/2,
                           (geodata.bounds['miny'].min()+geodata.bounds['maxy'].max())/2)
```

So if a list of geojson URLs was passed into `geo_path` they could be merged together? (This came up as a use case for me where I was trying to plot maps for an arbitrary number of administrative regions, each with their own geojson file.)

Adding in something to automatically set an appropriate zoom level based on bounds might also be handy?

I'm doing several things mixing folium with crossfilters and nvd3 these days.

As discussed in #300, it may worth externalizing things from folium for it to focus on Leaflet integration.

I would propose to split folium into:

* `folium` = the leaflet part
* `branca` = the `element + colormap` part

(any other name suggestion is welcome ; I think about branca because it means "branch" in latin and folium would be attached to it.)

`branca` would be a dependency of `folium` but they would both be maintained in `python-visualization` community.

A good timing for this split would be just after `v0.2` release ; provided we have few open PR.

Is it possible to treat a MarkerCluster as a normal (overlay) layer?

- Give it a name (the _name_ parameter listed in 0.2.0 docs is not supported in 0.3.0)
- Have it show up in the layer control (adding LayerControl to a map with a MarkerCluster does nothing)
- Configure color and styling of markers and cluster indicators
- Support multiple MarkerCluster layers on a single map (when I add three MarkerClusters to the same map I actually get three separate world maps)

I don't know if any of the above is already possible but assume that most of this would be feature requests that would take a bit of work.

Or maybe there's another way to get one or more layers containing point markers in a clustered view onto a map?

issue_title: move resources to the test folder
'/v1/environments/{0}/collections/{1}/notices' api not updating after deleting all training data
issue_body:
I've augmented python sdk to interact with discovery service api's for which support is missing the python sdk.

The methods added are for deleting all training data in a collection, querying for the set of training data defined on a collection, and querying for the set of notices available for a collection.

Subclass with implementation of relevant discovery service api interaction in the class below:

```py
class CustomDiscovery(DiscoveryV1):
    def delete_all_training_examples(self: DiscoveryV1,
                                     environment_id: str,
                                     collection_id: str):
        """Delete all training examples for collection
        """
        base_url = '/v1/environments/{0}/collections/{1}/training_data/'

        url_string = base_url.format(
            environment_id, collection_id)

        params = {'version': self.version}

        return self.request(method='DELETE',
                            url=url_string,
                            params=params
                            )

    def get_training_data(self: DiscoveryV1,
                          environment_id: str,
                          collection_id: str
                          ):
        """
        Get notices
        :param environment_id the guid of the environment
        :param collection_id collection guid
        :return dict of notice info
        """
        base_url = '/v1/environments/{0}/collections/{1}/training_data'

        url_string = base_url.format(
            environment_id, collection_id)

        params = {'version': self.version}

        return self.request(method='GET',
                            url=url_string,
                            params=params,
                            accept_json=True)

    def get_notices(self: DiscoveryV1,
                    environment_id: str,
                    collection_id: str
                    ):
        """
        Get notices
        :param environment_id the guid of the environment
        :param collection_id collection guid
        :return dict of notice info
        """
        base_url = '/v1/environments/{0}/collections/{1}/notices'

        url_string = base_url.format(
            environment_id, collection_id)

        params = {'version': self.version}

        return self.request(method='GET',
                            url=url_string,
                            params=params,
                            accept_json=True)
```

If I:

(1) add training examples which cause 'notices' (querying the set of training data confirms its not empty)
(2) then delete all training examples (re-querying the set of training data confirms its empty)
(3) then re-query the notices api -- the notices api still reports errors associated with the no longer present training data

Shouldn't the notices api update after the problematic training data is removed?

issue_title: Question about fields.Nested  and APISpec.definition() call order
issue_body:
What is correct way to handle nested schemas with apispec? For example I have something like that

```python
class BarSchema(Schema):
    baz = fields.String()


class FooSchema(Schema):
    bars = fields.Nested(BarSchema, many=True)


spec.definition('Foo', spec=FooSchema)
spec.definition('Bar', spec=BarSchema)
spec.to_dict()
```

Generated definition for Foo includes Bar as nested object instead of referencing existing definition. Changing order of schemas in spec helps
```python
spec.definition('Bar', spec=BarSchema)
spec.definition('Foo', spec=FooSchema)
```
but what about situations with circular dependencies when FooSchema nests BarSchema and vice versa?

issue_title: How to specify attributes (such as dump_only,...) on Nested fields?
Docs: requesting example of deserialization with fields.Method
issue_body:
From #394:

> `Method` and `Function` callbacks are not called if the field is missing in JSON

This was quite surprising to me, which told me that I had fundamentally misunderstood what these field types were for on the deserializing side.

For instance, going by the [custom fields `Method` example][custom-fields], I had 1) missed that this example was only about serializiation; 2) thought that a `Method` field would pull data from an already-deserialized object, and that doing so would create a new field; and 3) therefore thought that the `Method` field would never be expecting to parse data given by its field name; only to generate it from some other supplied data.. I now see that this is more like a transformation - it takes data present in the input and transforms it with the given method, storing the result in the deserialized object.

You can see how this misunderstanding led to me reporting this issue: sloria/webargs#94. I now realize that that case is probably better handled with a `@post_load` method. However, I'd like to request that examples be added showing deserialization with `Method` and `Function` fields, so that someone might not make this mistake in the future.

[custom-fields]: http://marshmallow.readthedocs.org/en/latest/custom_fields.html#method-fields

`Nested` field documentation says that the `default` parameter may be used to serialize `None` values:
`    :param default: Default value to if attribute is missing or None`
However, this does not correspond to reality: `default` values are only used to serialize missing fields. And it seems that there is no way to make the `default` value be used for serialization of `None` values — by fine-tuning the field parameters, at least.

The Python Software Foundation is dropping Python 2.7 support in 1.5 years (Jan 1, 2020) ([ref](https://devguide.python.org/#status-of-python-branches)). The major scientific python packages (numpy, pandas, matplotlib, scipy, etc.) are dropping Python 2.7 support in less than a year, though this is a little nuanced and depends on the package ([ref](https://python3statement.org/)). 

The question here is should we commit to a plan for dropping Python 3 support, or should we wait until it's unavoidable?

Here's a list of pros and cons that I see for moving pvlib to python 3 only and a date for dropping python 3 support. This is specific to pvlib and python 3, rather than python 3 overall...

Pros:

- We can advertise pvlib on https://python3statement.org/
- Remove a small amount of compatibility code
- keyword only arguments could be useful for some complicated pvlib function signatures
- Matrix multiplication operator (3.5+)
- Better unpacking (useful for kwargs magic) (3.5+)
- We can (maybe) use f-strings in pvlib (3.6+)
- dict retains ordering, so no need to use OrderedDict in pvlib (3.6+)
- Maybe type annotations (3.7+) but I think the scientific python community is going to have to struggle with this for a bit

Cons:

- It's an unnecessary burden for people that are tied to 2.7

Proposed dates:

- 2019-06-01 (@wholmgren)

I think these pros are small but meaningful, especially once we can commit to 3.6+, while the cons are unknown because I don't have a good understanding of how many pvlib users are on 2.7 and what might be holding them back. My understanding is that download statistics from pypi and anaconda are not very reliable for this question, but I will still try to compile some soon.

Again, this is a discussion about pvlib moving to python 3 only -- it is not a discussion about the merits of python 3 overall. For example, while the new Python 3.7 [``breakpoint``](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#breakpoint) function looks great, it's not going to be used within pvlib so it's irrelevant to the discussion. 

Anyone watching that is tied to 2.7 should speak now, because I think there are a handful of contributors that would like to push us to python 3 only sooner than later!

So, please post a date you'd like to see pvlib commit to Python 3+ only and list any additional pros and cons. I will update the lists as people post.

issue_title: bv_val("#x0...2", 256) returns expr with null m_ast member

issue_body:
Github doesn't allow me to re-open #70, but this is in the same vein.

Based on this definition from the "spec":

```
remε,σ : RM × Fε,σ × Fε,σ → Fε,σ
remε,σ(rm, f, g) = f                            if  g ∈ FIε,σ
                   NaN                          if  f ∈ FIε,σ ∪ {NaN}
                   NaN                          if  g ∈ FZε,σ ∪ {NaN}
                   rnd(v, rm, isNeg(f), x)      if  x = v(f) − (v(g) ∗ y),
                                                    y = rnd(in, rm, 0, v(f)/v(g))
```

I do believe fpRem should simply return the first argument if the second argument is an infinity.

Currently Z3 is returning NaN when the first argument is Infinity; instead of NaN. i.e.,

`fpRem +oo +oo` and `should evaluate to `+00`, but Z3 is currently producing `NaN`.

Same issue with `fpRem +oo -oo`; `fpRem -oo +oo`; and `fpRem -oo -oo`.

issue_title: some links in wiki are obsoleted
issue_body:
Hi, guys, 
it seems that some links in the wiki page(link:`https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/wiki/Slides`) like `Z3: An Efficient SMT Solver (PDF)` are obsoleted, the link address of `Z3: An Efficient SMT Solver (PDF)` is `http://research.microsoft.com/projects/z3/Z3_System.pdf`, and it just redirect to MS Research's home page now.....

My understanding is that `(check-sat-using smt)` should be equivalent to `(check-sat)`. This seems not to always be the case. I've found a couple of examples where they actually return different results (attached here: [z3-tactic-bug.zip](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/800453/z3-tactic-bug.zip)). For the first test case (`bug1.smt2` and `bug1-tactic.smt2`), `(check-sat)` returns `unknown`, while `(check-sat-using smt)` returns `unsat`. For bug 2 (`bug2.smt2` and `bug2-tactic.smt2`), the situation is reversed (though `(check-sat-using smt)` takes an extremely long time, around 8 minutes on my machine, to return `unknown`.

I ran Z3 with `-v:10` on bug 1, and observed some differences:

Using `(check-sat)`:
```
(smt.simplifier-done)
(smt.simplifier-done)
(smt.searching)
(smt.simplifying-clause-set :num-deleted-clauses 235)
(smt.restarting :propagations 9602 :decisions 421 :conflicts 101 :restart 150 :agility 0.080781)
(smt.simplifying-clause-set :num-deleted-clauses 17)
(smt.giveup quantifiers)
unknown
```

Using `(check-sat-using smt)`:
```
(smt.simplifier-done)
(smt.simplifier-done)
(smt.searching)
(smt.simplifying-clause-set :num-deleted-clauses 36)
(smt.restarting :propagations 7663 :decisions 537 :conflicts 101 :restart 150 :agility 0.0522954)
(smt.simplifying-clause-set :num-deleted-clauses 13)
unsat
```

It seems surprising that simplification is behaving so differently, and I wonder whether this is causing the difference in results.

I'm opening a separate issue about timing differences (`(check-sat-using smt)`) seems to be significantly slower in many cases).

In order to connect to any Jupyter kernel, they rely on zmq for reliable interprocess communication. The node bindings, which are quite stable (and awesome), are a bit persnickety to install on some platforms (as with anything relying on native bindings to C). In order for a developer to build, e.g., an Electron app that relies on zmq, they tend to need these items:

* zmq headers
* ~~(optionally) libsodium for encryption~~ TweetNACL is built in now!
* C compiler
* pkg-config
* for every version of Electron and node that come out, these need to be rebuilt
* rebuild for each version

[Releasing multiplatform precompiled binaries for node modules is completely doable](http://cylonjs.com/blog/2014/11/19/creating-multiplatform-precompiled-binaries-for-node-modules/), though care has to be taken to allow users to use their local version of libzmq + libsodium as well.

If you dig through issues on zmq, you'll find quite a few about [installation issues](https://github.com/JustinTulloss/zeromq.node/issues/360). Currently there are bundled Windows binaries *in* the repository for libzmq (for linking against).

A brief test of nteract's behavior when used with a screenreader exposes several bugs. It'd be great to have an accessibility specialist or a visually-impaired individual give the application an audit for its behavior on different screen-readers.

Furthermore, we should run our application through [react-a11y](https://github.com/reactjs/react-a11y) to find other accessibility issues that might contribute to making nteract play nicer with screen readers.

issue_title: DSE symbolic var: size of simfile implicitly restricted in size
issue_body:
Hi, 
When I'm running angr with a symbolic file and the size of the simfile is symbolic, I found out that symbolic execution doesn't work unless I use the value 8*8=64 bits. However, this is implicit and very unclear. printing simgr.errored showed me: ` State errored with "args' length must all be equal" `. 
Maybe an (implicit) convert or a general warning would be good to make sure people don't end up debugging for hours until they start randomly changing numbers or find out about simgr.errored :)

For completion, here is the code I'm talking about: 
`    # angr project
    self.project = angr.Project(self.binary )
    initstate = self.project.factory.entry_state(args=[self.binary, '/tmp/simfile'])
    
    # simfilesize
    simfilesize = claripy.BVS('simfilesize', 16*8) # 16*8 doesn't work 8*8 does
    initstate.solver.add(simfilesize > 0)
    initstate.solver.add(simfilesize < 64*8)  

    # simfile
    simfile = angr.SimFile('simfile', size=simfilesize)
    simfile.set_state(initstate)
    initstate.fs.insert('/tmp/simfile', simfile)
    
    simgr = self.project.factory.simgr(initstate)
    simgr.explore(find=self.find)
`

issue_title: ImageContainerQuick does not mask fill values in high resolution images
issue_body:
``ImageContainerQuick.resample()`` does not mask fill values in high resolution images. The output is a masked array, but its mask is ``False``. It works fine with low resolutions though. The problem seems to be independent of the projection (I didn't check all). 

I'm using pyresample-1.5.0 with numpy-1.13.0 .

Example:

    import numpy as np
    import pyresample.geometry
    import pyresample.image
    import pyresample.plot
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    from pycmsaf.geometry import get_proj_corners
    
    for nx, ny in zip([360, 3600], [180, 1800]):
        # Generate some test data with masked elements
        x = np.arange(nx, dtype='f4')
        y = np.arange(ny, dtype='f4')
        x *= 2 * np.pi / x.max()
        y *= 2 * np.pi / y.max()
        mx, my = np.meshgrid(x, y)
        data = np.sin(np.sqrt(mx ** 2 + my ** 2))
        data = np.ma.masked_where(data > 0.98, data)
    
        # Define regular lon-lat grid (lower left corner: [-180, -90], upper right
        # corner: [180, 90])
        regular_grid = pyresample.geometry.AreaDefinition(
            area_id='my_regular_grid',
            name='Regular 1x1 degree grid',
            proj_id='my_id',
            proj_dict={'proj': 'eqc', 'lon_0': 0},
            x_size=nx,
            y_size=ny,
            area_extent=[-20037508.342789244, -10018754.171394622,
                         20037508.342789244, 10018754.171394622])
    
        # Define web-mercator grid (lower left corner: [-180, -85], upper right
        # corner: [180, 85])
        proj4_str = '+proj=merc +a=6378137 +b=6378137 +lat_ts=0.0 +lon_0=0.0 ' \
                    '+x_0=0.0 +y_0=0 +k=1.0 +units=m +nadgrids=@null'
        web_mercator = pyresample.utils.get_area_def(
            area_id='my_web_mercator',
            area_name='Web Mercator',
            proj_id='my_id',
            proj4_args=proj4_str,
            x_size=ny,
            y_size=ny,
            area_extent=[-20037508.342789244, -19971868.88040857,
                         20037508.342789244, 19971868.880408563]
        )
    
        # Resample data from regular lon-lat grid to web-mercator grid
        con_reg = pyresample.image.ImageContainerQuick(
            image_data=data, geo_def=regular_grid, fill_value=None)
        con_merc = con_reg.resample(web_mercator)
        data_merc = con_merc.image_data
        print data_merc.mask
    
        # Plot data
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        bmap = pyresample.plot.area_def2basemap(web_mercator, resolution='c')
        bmap.ax = ax
        bmap.drawcoastlines()
        bmap.imshow(data_merc, origin='upper')
        ax.set_title('nx={0}, ny={1}'.format(nx, ny))
    
    plt.show()

issue_title: inputdata not always recorded for subjobs on disk
issue_body:
Hi all,

There are some users complaining due to the fact that they cannot resubmit failed subjobs because their subjobs don't have the inputdata recorded on disk.
These are typically LHCb/Gaudi/Dirac jobs which are split using SplitByFiles using the Offline splitter.
All within Ganga 6.1.11 I've been told.

I cannot reproduce this error from testing and am waiting an example script from a user to demonstrate how they're setting up their job. As I can't reproduce this after several attempts I'm really waiting on user input to actually determine what is causing this.

I'll update this if this develops.

Rob

Running the latest develop version, ```Ganga/Utilities/__init__.py``` and ```files.py``` are both shipped with the job, but no ```Ganga/__init__.py```, meaning that the job fails on the backend whenever attempting to load files relative to the _python/Ganga base directory (e.g. when adding an output sandbox).

From discussion with @chrismbeckett on IRC:

3) Orphaned data in the client db when permissions change.

So you sync a bunch of data based on current permissions, and then another user changes the permissions so that you no longer have rights to that data. However, it is still sitting in their client side IndexDB and never gets nor updated nor removed.

issue_title: Autocomplete fails because of related_model property
issue_body:
Hi, maybe is not a django-jet issue, but if I use `RelatedFieldAjaxListFilter` I get a server error because `related_model` is returning a string instead of a model instance.

I also reported this behaviour on stackoverflow because maybe is a Django issue:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35745607/why-djangos-related-model-property-is-returning-string-instead-of-model-instanc

A possible workaround is to directly get the related model through `field.remote_field.model`

    app_label = field.remote_field.model._meta.app_label
    model_name = field.remote_field.model._meta.object_name

I need to do this workaround for me and I can make a pull request if you think it is interesting to avoid future problems.

Thanks!

issue_title: citrination-client and plotly should be optional requirements
issue_body:
@saurabh02 

Commit 948fe5e96ff51469d54914fe0dacc072aa217037 makes citrination-client and plotly required libraries of matminer. This should not be the case. We can think of a typical use case of matminer as someone that has a spreadsheet of data and wants to add descriptors (composition or structure) to it and then run a data mining model. Neither plotly nor citrination-client is needed for that (although pymatgen and pandas are). If a library is extremely lightweight or robust then it's also usually OK to put it in requirements.

As far as I can tell you added the requirements to get a unit test to pass. But the way to do it is to fix the unit test, not require additional libraries. e.g., pymatgen contains examples on how to write unittests with optional libraries (you raise a SkipTest if the library is not installed). Can you try it this way?
 
Note also that if you do add requirements, you should also pin the version as per the other requirements.
Thanks for this project! This is not an issue, but more like a feature request: would you be interested in adding more sources of data (e.g. Nomad, Materials Cloud etc.)?

After installing with `pip3 install msk` I ran the command `msk create`. This error occurred (attached file). Can you please tell why this is happening? 
[error.txt](https://github.com/MycroftAI/mycroft-skills-kit/files/2061098/error.txt)

Old-style simulations we've been using on neurosim include easy-to-read tables (wmat for weights, pmat for probability of connections, ...). These tables are typically indexed by cell type. Eg wmat[type1][type2] = 2 - means weight from type1 -> type2 is 2.0. NetPyne's syntax is more flexible/powerful but perhaps these tables are easier to read at a glance, with fast lookup of desired values via indexing. So - feature request is to have conversion to these types of tables (this is already possible given drawing of connection weights between types).  

some commonly used tables:
synloc - synaptic location (perhaps not general enough)
delm (delays)
wmatex - external weights
wmat - weights
pmat - probability of connections
numc - number of cells of  atype
ix,ixe - starting/ending IDs of cells (indexed by cell type)
allcells, icells, ecells - scalars representing # of cells in network and I and E cells

some tables could have added flexibility by combining with dictionaries...

I have seen that the `info` parameter in the `resolve` function provides a `info.field_asts`, which provides information about the selected fields, but when fragments are provided you get something like:

```py
[Field(alias=None, name=Name(value=u'customer'), arguments=[], directives=[], selection_set=SelectionSet(selections=[Field(alias=None, name=Name(value=u'id'), arguments=[], directives=[], selection_set=None), FragmentSpread(name=Name(value=u'__RelayQueryFragment0wau8gf'), directives=[])]))]
```

issue_title: Slicing is ignored when using DjangoFilterConnectionField with a custom resolver
Mutations with Django User as SimpleLazyObject throw an error
issue_body:
Upgrading to graphene 1.0 and graphene-django, a mutation I have started throwing an error. *Note, queries don't throw the error.* None of the django examples incorporate a user object, one with a mutation would be great.

`Received incompatible instance "use@example.com"."`

https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene-django/blob/master/graphene_django/types.py#L105

I started debugging and found that Django is using the `SimpleLazyObject` to represent users since they may or may not be anonymous, which is causing `DjangoObjectType.is_type_of` to throw the error.

The call to `is_valid_django_model` doesn't pass since SimpeLazyObject isn't a class according to `inspect.isclass(model)` and the error is raised. 

I thought about trying to cast the SimpleLazyObject to the User, [talked about here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/11314905/request-user-returns-a-simplelazyobject-how-do-i-wake-it), but decided to just override the `is_type_of` classmethod for my `UserNode`.

I'm not super happy with this, since I'm not sure what `is_valid_django_model` is trying to catch.

```py
class UserNode(DjangoObjectType):
    """Graphene object for User."""

    class Meta:
        model = User
        only_fields = ('first_name', 'last_name', 'email', 'profile')
        filter_fields = ['first_name', 'last_name', 'email']
        filter_order_by = ['first_name']
        interfaces = (Node, )

    @classmethod
    def is_type_of(cls, root, context, info):
        """Override `is_type_of` to take care of django's SimpleLazyObject for the User model in mutations."""
        if isinstance(root, cls):
            return True
        model = root._meta.model
        return model == cls._meta.model
```

Here's the mutation (stripped down) that was throwing the error:

```py
from graphql_relay.node.node import from_global_id
Rid = namedtuple('Rid', 'name id')

class IntroduceAirplane(relay.ClientIDMutation):
    """Mutation to add and edit airplanes"""

    class Input:
        name = graphene.String()
        id = graphene.String()

    credit_app = graphene.Field(CreditAppNode)

    @classmethod
    def mutate_and_get_payload(cls, input, context, info):
        """Method to clean the input and create a rebate."""
        if not context.user.is_authenticated():
            raise Exception('User is not logged in.')

        pk = input.get('id')

        if pk:
            rid = Rid(*from_global_id(pk))
            input.pop('id', None)
            Airplane.objects.filter(pk=rid.id).update(**input)
            airplane = Airplane.objects.get(pk=rid.id)
        else:
            airplane = Airplane.objects.create(created_by=context.user, **input)

        return IntroduceAirplane(airplane=airplane)
```

issue_title: Intercept redirects will cause ContentNotRenderedError with django-debug-toolbar 1.9
issue_body:
I tried newest django-debug-toolbar, 1.9.
And when I enabled Intercept Redirects and accessed a view returns 302, It returned `ContentNotRenderedError`:

```
ContentNotRenderedError at /admin/

The response content must be rendered before it can be accessed.

Request Method: 	GET
Request URL: 	http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/
Django Version: 	1.11.7
Exception Type: 	ContentNotRenderedError
Exception Value: 	

The response content must be rendered before it can be accessed.

Exception Location: 	/tmp/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/template/response.py in content, line 129
Python Executable: 	/tmp/venv/bin/python
Python Version: 	3.6.3
Python Path: 	

['/tmp/myproj',
 '/usr/lib/python36.zip',
 '/usr/lib/python3.6',
 '/usr/lib/python3.6/lib-dynload',
 '/tmp/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages']

Server time: 	Wed, 15 Nov 2017 11:17:34 +0000

Environment:


Request Method: GET
Request URL: http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/

Django Version: 1.11.7
Python Version: 3.6.3
Installed Applications:
['django.contrib.admin',
 'django.contrib.auth',
 'django.contrib.contenttypes',
 'django.contrib.sessions',
 'django.contrib.messages',
 'django.contrib.staticfiles',
 'debug_toolbar']
Installed Middleware:
['debug_toolbar.middleware.DebugToolbarMiddleware',
 'django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware',
 'django.contrib.sessions.middleware.SessionMiddleware',
 'django.middleware.common.CommonMiddleware',
 'django.middleware.csrf.CsrfViewMiddleware',
 'django.contrib.auth.middleware.AuthenticationMiddleware',
 'django.contrib.messages.middleware.MessageMiddleware',
 'django.middleware.clickjacking.XFrameOptionsMiddleware']



Traceback:

File "/tmp/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/core/handlers/exception.py" in inner
  41.             response = get_response(request)

File "/tmp/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/utils/deprecation.py" in __call__
  142.             response = self.process_response(request, response)

File "/tmp/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/debug_toolbar/middleware.py" in process_response
  125.         content = force_text(response.content, encoding=response.charset)

File "/tmp/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/template/response.py" in content
  129.                 'The response content must be rendered before it can be accessed.'

Exception Type: ContentNotRenderedError at /admin/
Exception Value: The response content must be rendered before it can be accessed.
```

## Reason

This error caused by `content = force_text(response.content, encoding=response.charset)`, accessing `response.content`.

In this case the `response` was returned by `RedirectsPanel`.
https://github.com/jazzband/django-debug-toolbar/blob/master/debug_toolbar/panels/redirects.py#L9

The panel returns `SimpleTemplateResponse` object. this class will raise `ContentNotRenderedError` when `response.content` was accessed before calling `response.render()`.
like `content = force_text(response.content, ...)`.

This error wasn't on django-debug-toolbar 1.8.
And I think this change caused it
https://github.com/jazzband/django-debug-toolbar/commit/c369384945cfc5a4e65fa5f153e231a7ec06b11b

## Fix idea

Simply, we can avoid this error by adding `response.render()` in `RedirectsPanel`,
folloing here https://github.com/jazzband/django-debug-toolbar/blob/master/debug_toolbar/panels/redirects.py#L27

like this.

```python
                # Using SimpleTemplateResponse avoids running global context processors.
                response = SimpleTemplateResponse('debug_toolbar/redirect.html', context)
                response.cookies = cookies
                response.render()
```

Or any other nice plan? (or this issue was not correct?)
I don't know well about codes of django-debug-toolbar, so I didn't create PR yet.
Please ask and tell me anything.

issue_title: Prioritization of MLE messages over data and handling of child re-attach
issue_body:
Summarizing two features: 

- Prioritize MLE traffic over data packet. This is particularly important for MLE "Child Id Response" message. 

- Current implementation removes the child/neighbor when we recive a "Parent Requst" from it (child tries to re-attahc). This in turn causes all queued data packets for the node to be cleared. It'd be better to keep all the data packets  but clear out the queued MLE packets.

Chages would be as follows:
- Add otCoapHeaderGetFirstOption - this allows for the application to do it's processing (e.g. query options).
- Remove otCoapHeaderGetCurrentOption - it becomes redundant.
- In Server::ProcessReceivedMessage, in option parsing change default (unknown option) behavior from ExitNow(), to break (i.e. continue parsing options). This is the second change needed to allow for the application to parse options.

My primary concern is with the default behavior in ProcessReceivedMessage. Was it chosen for any particular reason?

issue_title: MbedTls failure during commissioning on slow platforms.
issue_body:
We're seeing issues when running the OpenThread stack on an older platform, where the crypto computations are done on the CPU and are not fast enough to avoid DTLS timeouts on the other side.

The issues are similar to, but not the same as, what is reported in: https://github.com/openthread/openthread/pull/1207

In our case the code (including the fix "0001-Fix-commissioning-problem-with-retransmissions.patch") behaves as follows: 

Note that in my setup the Joiner is the slow platform which takes more than 8 seconds to perform it’s computations, whereas the Commissioner is on a faster platform (therefor there are no repeats of the 'Client Hello' messages).

```
   Joiner                               Commissioner
(1)  |              Client Hello              |
     |--------------------------------------->|
     |                                        |
(2)  |          Hello Verify Request          |
     |<---------------------------------------|
     |                                        |
(3)  |         Client Hello + cookie          |
     |--------------------------------------->|
     |                                        |
(4)  |              Server Hello              |
     |          Server Key Exchange           |
     |           Server Hello Done            |
     |<---------------------------------------|
     |                                        | |
(5)  |              Server Hello              | |  DTLS timeout
     |          Server Key Exchange           | |
     |           Server Hello Done            | v
     |<---------------------------------------| 
     |                                        |
(6)  |          Client Key Exchange           |
     |           Change Cipher Spec           |
     |      Encrypted Handshake Message       |
     |        Client Key Exchange (DUP)       |
     |        Change Cipher Spec (DUP)        |
     |   Encrypted Handshake Message (DUP)    |
     |--------------------------------------->|
     |                                        |
(7)  |           Change Cipher Spec           |
     |      Encrypted Handshake Message       |
     |<---------------------------------------|
     |                                        |
(8)  |            Application Data            |
     |--------------------------------------->|
     |                                        |
(9)  |        Change Cipher Spec (DUP)        |
     |   Encrypted Handshake Message (DUP)    |
     |         Application Data (DUP)         |
     |<---------------------------------------|
     |                                        |

```

Because the Joiner takes a long time to compute the data for (6), one sees that on the Commissioner side the 8s timeout gets triggered, and the previous message (4) is retransmitted as (5).

After creating the data for (6) the TLS code notices that there are repeat messages,
and decides (as per spec) to reply to both (4) and (5). In our case both replies end up in the same message (6) !

Now, the Commissioner replies with (7), but only removes the first half of (6) out of its rx queue.

When the Commissioner receives (8), it notices the remaining part of (6) is still in its rx queue, and replies to this first with the "Change Cipher Spec (DUP)". Additionally,  it also replies to the recently received Application Data (8). Both replies end up in (9).

At this point, the Joiner code has moved to a state where it's looking for "Application Data", but there is unexpected data (Change Cipher Spec (DUP)) in front of it.
The current DTLS code doesn't handle this situation: it sees an incorrect record and discards the complete UDP packet (and not only the offending record).


That's the current situation. It is clear that the current patch does only fixes a single variation of the problem (again, see PR1207), and not the bigger problem of the DTLS state machine ending up in an incorrect state.

There are multiple paths to fix the underlying problem:
* one could adapt the code to also handle the "Change Cipher Spec" by trplying to it (I've tried it and it works). But this requires again multiple fixes, and I don't think this is the correct way forward: in fact it's like handling multiple parallel handshakes. The sniffer logs end up looking very messy with all those (unnecessary) retransmission. Also, it spins out of control, with multiple retransmissions on both sides when two older platforms start a DTLS session between one another.

* we should reconsider sending the initial retransmit of the "Client Key Exchange".
The spec clearly states it should be done:

>     "2. The implementation reads a retransmitted flight from the peer: the
>       implementation transitions to the SENDING state, where it
>       retransmits the flight, resets the retransmit timer, and returns
>       to the WAITING state.  The rationale here is that the receipt of a
>       duplicate message is the likely result of timer expiry on the peer
>       and therefore suggests that part of one's previous flight was
>       lost."
> 

But, it also mentions that the reasoning behind this rule is to retransmit a flight that was previously lost. In the outlined case there is no previous flight. When one runs a slow platform, it's obvious that the retransmits are NOT caused by a lost previous flight, so why send multiple replies in that case ?


The code that triggers this type of retransmit is located in third_party/mbedtls/repo/library/ssl_tls.c:

```
int mbedtls_ssl_prepare_handshake_record( mbedtls_ssl_context *ssl ) {
...
            if( recv_msg_seq == ssl->handshake->in_flight_start_seq - 1 &&
                ssl->in_msg[0] != MBEDTLS_SSL_HS_HELLO_VERIFY_REQUEST )

```
To still benefit from the speed improvements, it is probably not required to entirely remove this retransmission mechanism. Only remove the retransmissions 
* @ compile time: only for slow platforms
* @ run time: only for the state(s) with known long computational delays.

Note: disabling this code will not create issues in case were a message really gets lost, because there is always the local timer which will timeout when no reply is received. This, in turn will (locally) trigger a retransmit.

Test_5_3_9 in Harness R14:  
> The purpose of this test case is to validate that the DUT is able to generate Address Query and Address Notification messages properly.

However the address should be configured via DHCP and Leader configured as Border Router with DHCPv6 Server.

**Observation:** The parent of the dut sends a icmp destination unreachable.

**Cause:** the ping destination is the mleid of the dut, but the mesh header final destination is the dut's parent's rloc16. Because the dut is a FED, the parent does not know the dut's mleid, therefore sending the icmp error (it can not ip forward the message).

**Down the rabbit hole:** the Final destination in the mesh header is the dut's parent's rloc16. I believe this should be the dut's rloc16. The preceding energy request is supposed to have the same source and destination as the ping, but it hasn't. On inspection, the mesh header of the energy request has as final destination the dut's rloc16. So why is the addressing of the energy request and the ping request different? I believe the answer lies in the energy response. The dut sends this response to its parent (_ip route over_). The parent then forwards (_mesh under_) it to the commissioner (*). The commissioner sees in the mesh header the dut's parent's rloc16 associated with the dut's mleid. I think the commissioner updates its eid-to-rloc cache with this (incorrect) information. Consequentially, it uses this incorrect information to send the ping request.
Attached are the wireshark and test harness logs
[logging.zip](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/files/1047563/logging.zip)


(*) I think this behaviour is incorrect: the dut should use a mesh header with O=dut's rloc16 and F=commissioner's rloc16

issue_title: Generate a unicode sequence on Python  2.7
issue_body:
I use tensorflow (1.0.1) and tflearn (0.3) with Python 2.7
This is a code snippet from the function `generate`, in  `generator.py` (lines 220 - 228):
```python
	if type(sequence) == str:
	    generated += next_char
	    sequence = sequence[1:] + next_char
	    whole_sequence += next_char
	else:
	    generated.append(next_char)
	    sequence = sequence[1:]
	    sequence.append(next_char)
	    whole_sequence.append(next_char)
```
When `sequence` is `unicode`, the condition goes to the `else` part, and since `unicode` does not a method `append`, I get the following exception:
`AttributeError: 'unicode' object has no attribute 'append'`
Of course this does not happen in Python 3. To fix it, I suggest to simply add a condition to check if the sequence is of type `unicode`.

Functions take a win (window) argument but the documentation refers to windows as panes, and the window id returned is "pane_UID". 
It's inconsistent and confusing.

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Please update the react-grid-layout version. The new version has a few bug fixes, which fixes quite annoying bugs.

issue_title: Change VSTS configuration to use YAML build definitions
issue_body:
When you define a CI build on VSTS, you've got a fundamental choice: use a web-based interface or configure your CI process as code in a YAML build:
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/vsts/pipelines/build/yaml?view=vsts

YAML build pipelines give you the advantages of configuration as code.  However, this is an early preview feature which isn't 100% done yet and hasn't reached feature-parity with the web-based interface for configuring builds.

At some point we want to switch to the YAML-based VSTS build because it will make maintaining the build configuration easier.

For the 0.8.0 release of ``cmd2`` on February 1, 2018 we officially deprecated all usage of the **optparse** module within ``cmd2`` and replaced it with **argparse**-based decorators.

We should plan to delete the deprecated **optparse** code in either the 0.8.3 or 0.8.4 release.

Any opinions regarding which of these releases it should be deprecated in?

issue_title: pybind11 doesn't seem to honor `operator new()` (e.g. for Eigen aligned allocators)
issue_body:
#### Issue description

I would like to expose classes in Python which have Eigen matrices as fields. Such classes need to be [allocated aligned to 16 bytes for vectorized operations](https://eigen.tuxfamily.org/dox/group__TopicStructHavingEigenMembers.html). A canonical way of ensuring this is to use Eigen's "operator new" implementation via the macro `EIGEN_MAKE_ALIGNED_OPERATOR_NEW`.

However it seems that pybind11 doesn't honor the alignment-aware `operator new()` when exposing such a class to Python, causing segfaults. Crashes seem to appear only when compiling with vectorized operations enabled.

This may be a feature request if pybind11 currently does not support a custom `operator new()`. In this case, could this be mentioned in the docs and/or a compile-time error be generated? It would be very helpful instead of having to trace the reason of a segfault.

Thanks a lot for your time and for the great tool!

#### Reproducible example code

See attachment [pybind11_eigenaligned.tar.gz](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/files/856416/pybind11_eigenaligned.tar.gz). I'm assuming the host system has vectorized operations available via `-march=native`. Then, in python:

    >>> import pybind11_eigenaligned
    >>> pybind11_eigenaligned.Data().showB()
    Assertion failed: ((internal::UIntPtr(array) & (31)) == 0 && "this assertion is explained here: " "http://eigen.tuxfamily.org/dox-devel/group__TopicUnalignedArrayAssert.html" " **** READ THIS WEB PAGE !!! ****"), function plain_array, file /usr/local/include/eigen3/Eigen/src/Core/DenseStorage.h, line 128.
    Abort trap: 6

I'm running Mac OS Sierra with Apple's clang and Eigen 3.3.3. The problem occurs both with pybind11 2.0.1 as well as current pybind11 git master.

issue_title: keep_alive<0,1>() fails for method where the return value may sometimes validly be nullptr
issue_body:
If keep_alive<0,1> is used a RuntimeError("Could not allocate weak reference!") exception is thrown when nullptr is returned. If keep_alive<0,1> is not used we have a use-after-free bug, and possible segfault.

Minimal example:
[example-341.py.txt](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/files/421455/example-341.py.txt)
[example-341.cpp.txt](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/files/421456/example-341.cpp.txt)

This problem is fixed by https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/pull/341

issue_title: Allow access to static class variables that are defined in the header file
issue_body:
We have C++ classes with static constants (such as ints) whose value is specified in the header file. When we try to wrap these with pybind11 we find that the code fails at run time with a failure to find the symbol.

Presumably this is because the address cannot be taken of the constant if its value is specified in the header instead of in the implementation file.

I am hoping you might be able to find a way to fix this (e.g. by making a copy?). We have the luxury of modifying most of our C++ code, so it has been primarily a nuisance for us. But I can imagine wanting to wrap C++ code cannot be modified, at which point the problem becomes more difficult.

issue_title: How to wrap namespaces wit pybind11?

issue_body:
## Issue description

The definition of `pybind11:slice` does not allow one to create a slice object where one of start, stop, step are None, since the only constructor requires all three be of type ``ssize_t``.

From https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/pytypes.h#L1062:

```c++
    slice(ssize_t start_, ssize_t stop_, ssize_t step_) {
        int_ start(start_), stop(stop_), step(step_);
        m_ptr = PySlice_New(start.ptr(), stop.ptr(), step.ptr());
        if (!m_ptr) pybind11_fail("Could not allocate slice object!");
    }
```

Slicing notation places uses None whenever the index is variable, e.g. `somelist[:]` means `slice(None, None, None)`. 

Specifically one cannot create a slice that asks for all elements of a list reversed without constructing a slice that has `stop=None`.

```python
>>> l = [1,2,3,4,5]
>>> l[::-1]  # Requires start=None, stop=None, step=-1
[5,4,3,2,1]
>>> l[5:0:-1]
[5,4,3,2]  # stop=0 terminates the list *before* the 0th element
>>> l[5:-1:-1]
[] # stop=-1 terminates the list before the final element
```

Of course PySlice_New can be called but the pybind API is quite nice :).

issue_title: DNS label too long message is inaccurate
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce:

`certbot -d .foo.com`

`Requested domain .foo.com is not a FQDN because label  is too long.`

Problems:

Initial label is actually too short (zero length).
Two spaces after `label` and before `is too`.
We should probably say, instead of FQDN, "is not a valid domain name."

Maybe just remove this check altogether? The server does this check pretty early on.

```
    # FQDN checks according to RFC 2181: domain name should be less than 255
    # octets (inclusive). And each label is 1 - 63 octets (inclusive).
    # https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2181#section-11
    msg = "Requested domain {0} is not a FQDN because ".format(domain)
    labels = domain.split('.')
    for l in labels:
        if not 0 < len(l) < 64:
            raise errors.ConfigurationError(msg + "label {0} is too long.".format(l))
    if len(domain) > 255:
        raise errors.ConfigurationError(msg + "it is too long.")
```

issue_title: --cert-path prepends a numeric prefix to the filename
issue_body:
When using `--csr` and `--cert-path`, the certificate is placed in a different location than what was specified by the user.

```
root@connor:~# /usr/local/bin/openssl req -new -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout dank.ninja.key -nodes -subj "/CN=dank.ninja" -reqexts SAN -config <( cat /usr/local/etc/ssl/openssl.cnf; echo "[SAN]"; echo subjectAltName=DNS:dank.ninja,DNS:www.dank.ninja ) -outform der -out dank.ninja.csr
Generating a 2048 bit RSA private key
.......................+++
....................................................+++
writing new private key to 'dank.ninja'
-----
```
```
root@connor:~# letsencrypt certonly --csr dank.ninja.csr --cert-path dank.ninja.crt

IMPORTANT NOTES:
 - Congratulations! Your certificate and chain have been saved at
   /root/0001_chain.pem. Your cert will expire on 2016-02-12. To
   obtain a new version of the certificate in the future, simply run
   Let's Encrypt again.
```
```
root@connor:~# ls dank.ninja.crt
ls: dank.ninja.crt: No such file or directory
```
```
root@connor:~# ls 0000_dank.ninja.crt
0000_dank.ninja.crt
```
