The apio init command generates the SConstruct, so that the user can change/modify it

For multiboard support, it is better to use the init comand for creating a new project, the same as platformio do:

apio init --board icestick

For that reason, the current apio init behaviour should be moved to apio scons:

apio scons    --> It will generate the default SConstruct file
apio init --> It will create the apio.ini file, with the project configuration: board, name, and so on...

In order for the atom build command to detect if the an apio build has succeed or not, apio should return error code (an error code diferent from 0 means no success)

It can be checked in the bash with the $? systema variable:

$ apio build
$ echo $?
0

Apio always return 0 (if does not matter if there was an error when building)

On the contrary, pio returns 0 when the build success, or any other code different than zero if the was an error

Ex:
$ pio run
Error: Not a PlatformIO project. `platformio.ini` file has not been found in current working directory (/home/obijuan/develop/test/pio1/src). To initialize new project please use `platformio init` command
$ echo $?
1

issue_title: handle expired messages in protocol queue
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Raiden protocol messages are ordered to maintain the monotonicity property of the messages, it may be the case that a locked transfer is added to the queue while the receiving node is offline, the node may come back online *after* the message in the queue has expired. The protocol won't be able to progress, since the newest message is the queue is always going to be invalid.

## Solution

Either recompute the messages in the queue or allow a node to receive messages with expired locks

System should be able to rebalance between channels. 

E.g. Alice has a channel with Bob and Charlie. One is almost exhausted while the other has a large capacity. She then wants to be able to make a transfer Alice > Charlie > .... > Bob > Alice.

issue_title: benchbuild fails catastrophically database connection fails.
issue_body: Benchbuild should not fail catastrophically when we cannot establish a database connection.
The output of `%preview --workflow` is plain text, which is difficult to read. We should include syntax highlighting for the output.

Currently tasks are executed as isolated blocks of statements that do not return any value to the step. Suppose we want to perform some tasks sequentially and stop the step under certain conditions, e.g.

```
input: for_each='rep'
stop_if( sigma < 0.005)
task: concurrent = False
    simulation to calculate sigma
```
there should be someway for us to return variables to sos, something like
```
input: for_each='rep'
stop_if( sigma < 0.005)
task: concurrent = False, shared='sigma'
sh: 
    simulation to calculate sigma
sigma = float(open(result).read())
```

where SoS would get the value of variable `sigma` from the task namespace, and return to sos.

Another example would be running a large number of tasks and calculate statistics of some quantity, in which case tasks could be executed in parallel.

Of course the biggest issue is that there can be many instances of `task` so the results should be a list, or a dictionary with `_index` as keys. Then, if we are to expose the result to the entire workflow, another `shared` option would be needed at the step level.

I'm running into various issues with `task` that I should report soon. But still I think for computations running on local desktop I'd rather not use `task` because it is otherwise much simpler to manage (I'll not have to worry about status or zombie processes). That'd essentially be what we have before (in the last release). The issue now is that without task I can only run it as a single thread process. I'm wondering if we can easily allow for multiple thread in that case -- basically ask non-task jobs to honor `-j`.

Several issues here #744 #753 #762 are in fact related to improper installation of dependency packages. I wonder if we should include an option in `setup.py` to install all these extra packages, and also document these dependencies?

We currently support eight languages (Bash, R, Python2, Python3, JavaScript, SAS, Matlab, Octave) that I suppose would be enough for > 90% people in bioinformatics / biostatistics and related fields. I have no plan to add another language before SoS 1.0 but anyone can suggest new languages/kernels to support before or after 1.0.

Candidates can include
* [ruby/iRuby]( https://github.com/SciRuby/iruby)
* [perl/iperl](https://github.com/EntropyOrg/p5-Devel-IPerl)
* [Julia/iJulia](https://github.com/JuliaLang/IJulia.jl)
* [sciLab/scilab_kernel](http://www.scilab.org/)
* ...

[TypeScript/iTypeScript](https://github.com/nearbydelta/itypescript) could be a low-hanging fruit because it is very similar to JavaScript.

issue_body:
Here are models and factories which I implemented referring to the [document](http://factoryboy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#relatedfactory) of `RelatedFactory`.

models.py

```python
from django.db import models


class City(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    capital_of = models.ForeignKey('factory_test.Country', on_delete=models.CASCADE)


class Country(models.Model):
    lang = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    capital_city = models.ForeignKey('factory_test.City', on_delete=models.CASCADE, null=True)
```

factories.py
```python
from factory.django import DjangoModelFactory

from . import models


class CityFactory(DjangoModelFactory):
    class Meta:
        model = models.City

    capital_of = None
    name = "Tronto"


class CountryFactory(DjangoModelFactory):
    class Meta:
        model = models.Country

    lang = 'fr'
    capital_city = factory.RelatedFactory(CityFactory, 'capital_of', name='Paris')

```

I expect that a `City` object is set to `county.capital_city` if I execute `country = CountryFactory()` but actual value is `None` instead.

Is this intended behaviour? I'm not sure but I wonder an object built by RelatedFactory should be set to parent instance like [this](https://github.com/l3msh0/factory_boy/commit/3aa4ebe6e025434fab27a4aabf3698964e2a3812).

issue_title: Transform is not thread safe if tfm_y=TfmType.PIXEL
issue_body:
Have a look at this line:
https://github.com/fastai/fastai/blob/13a8b6247c6278e1a56d5790beafcd6592639301/fastai/transforms.py#L172-L175

You assume that method `__call__` can be potentially accessed from multiple threads, hence you added the `RLock` there.  But as soon as `set_state` finishes the thread go to next line and the lock is freed, leaving the state open for further changes. Unfortunately the next line:
https://github.com/fastai/fastai/blob/13a8b6247c6278e1a56d5790beafcd6592639301/fastai/transforms.py#L181-L183

Assumes that the set_state stays unchanged during the whole execution, this is how RandomCrop is able to Crop both x and y in the same way. Unfortunately, the state can be changed since the `self.lock` was released.

To fix that  we should guard the whole execution like this:
```
 with self.lock:
      self.set_state()
      x,y = ((self.transform(x),y) if self.tfm_y==TfmType.NO
              else self.transform(x,y) if self.tfm_y==TfmType.PIXEL
              else self.transform_coord(x,y)) 
```

But that if I'm not mistaken that will kill any benefits that we have from having the transformations run in parallel.

The best would be to either pass state as paramters or use thread local variables (https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1408171/thread-local-storage-in-python).

I can propose a fix but since there is no unit test I'm not sure if I won't break anything. I'm trying to use the TfmType.PIXEL to create UNet implementation for the DataBowl, so once I have this somehow fixed I will add tests and then create PR.
i.e. numpy.random.choice is slow. Implement own.

issue_title: Boolean flags mismanaged during course creation and update
issue_body:
Working with `canvasapi` 0.6.0, I recently used `Course.create_assignment` to create a large number of assignments. Each assignment had both `peer_reviews` and `omit_from_final_grade` explicitly set to `False`. (Granted, these are the defaults, but for unrelated reasons it was useful to be explicit.) I was surprised to find that the newly-generated courses had each of these settings turned on, rather than off.

I then used `Assignment.edit` to correct the bad settings, again with both `peer_reviews` and `omit_from_final_grade` explicitly set to `False`. The calls reported no errors, but these two settings remained `True` on subsequent queries or when viewed in the standard web interface.

Next I used `Assignment.edit` to correct the bad settings again, but this time with both `peer_reviews` and `omit_from_final_grade` explicitly set to `'false'` as a lower-case string. The calls reported no errors, and the two settings were indeed changed as desired.

So it seems that `False` is either ignored or misinterpreted as true, whereas `'false'` is interpreted as false. I can pass the `'false'` string for now as a workaround, but surely it would be better for `False` to have the expected interpretation. When getting results back from the server, Boolean settings come back as `True` and `False`, not `'true'` and `'false'`. Passing settings up to the server in creation and modification requests should behave the same.

issue_title: CLI: Specifying --virtual-switch when creating NICs backed by OSA is cumbersome
issue_body:
### Actual behavior

When creating a NIC, the zhmc CLI currently requires the use of the `--adapter` and `--port` options for ROCE adapters, and the use of the `--virtual-switch` option for OSA and Hipersockets adapters. 

Determining the name of the correct virtual switch is cumbersome because (without a naming convention that allows identifying the adapter) the CLI user has to list all virtual switches and needs to show each of them until the one with the desired backing adapter is found. An additional complexity is that the backing adapter is shown in the virtual switch properties only with its URI, so the user needs to compare URIs in this process.

### Expected behavior

In the zhmc-ansible project, we have eliminated the virtual switch from the configuration input parameters, and both ROCE and OSA/HS adapters can be configured by specifying adapter name and port index.

It is suggested to implement a similar approach also for the CLI, i.e. to add support for using the `--adapter` and `--port` options also for OSA and Hipersockets adapters.

For backwards compatibility, we should continue supporting the `--virtual-switch` option for OSA and Hipersockets adapters. Specifying both the old and new options should be rejected.

### Execution environment

* zhmcclient version: 0.14.0
* Operating system (type+version): any
* HMC version: any
* CPC version: any

issue_title: Crispy filter does not include form media after 1.6.0 release
issue_body:
The crispy filter does not include a form's media anymore since release 1.6.0. This appears to be a bug as in the previous release, 1.5.2, the crispy filter when applied to a form or formset's context variable would include the form's media when rendered. **In the template** ``` {% load crispy_forms_filters %} ... {{ form|crispy }} ``` **Form class** ``` class MyForm(forms.Form): date=forms.DateField( label='Approved', required=True, widget=DatePlaceholder, ) ... ``` The date field uses a widget that requires some javaScript and CSS to work correctly. These files are included in the form's media when constructed in the view. In version 1.5.2, this media would be included in the rendered html as seen below: ```
``` However, when using version 1.6.0, without changing any code, this is the rendered html: ```
``` As you can see, the form media is no longer being included when being rendered by the filter. This seems like it is a bug introduced between release 1.5.2 and 1.6.0. It initially seems like this could be related to the changes from #525 with the optional inclusion of form media when using the `crispy` template tag. Perhaps the include_media context variable is not being set correctly.

issue_title: Testing for ticket actions in staff.py
issue_body:
Currently the tests for possible actions on tickets are rudimentary and dispersed:
- [test_ticket_submission.py](https://github.com/django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk/blob/master/helpdesk/tests/test_ticket_submission.py) tests creation of tickets, but leaves many sections in [staff.py](https://github.com/django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk/blob/master/helpdesk/views/staff.py) untouched.
- I recently added [test_ticket_actions](https://github.com/django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk/blob/master/helpdesk/tests/test_ticket_actions.py) to test for e.g. deletion, update and creation

Test coverage for [staff.py](https://github.com/django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk/blob/master/helpdesk/views/staff.py) and related ticket functionality should be extended. For this we should refactor these two files into proper unit tests for the ``staff.py`` views, with the goal of increasing the test coverage. 

This is a little matter of taste, but one could possibly avoid the django.test.Client() but use instead [properly constructed requests](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/testing/advanced/#django.test.RequestFactory) to do proper testing of all view functions (and their branches).

`setPhysicsCallback(physicsFnPtr)` in [`_gfs_dynamics.pyx`](https://github.com/CliMT/climt/blob/develop/climt/_components/gfs/_gfs_dynamics.pyx#L73) doesn't seem to be called by its component. Should it be removed?

issue_title: Idea: have a _view_count for all widgets
issue_body:
For performance reasons I need to know the number of views. I know @SylvainCorlay doesn't like exposing this. But let me give some use case(s).

Say I have at Output widget that will catch the output of matplotlib, that is the result of a really computational expensive result (yeah, vaex). Some external event, say a slider, or anything else can trigger a redraw of this plot, which might take several seconds. 
Now I reexecute the cell, so that the previous Output widget (or actually its view) is gone, although I still have a reference to it somewhere. Now the some event will trigger a redraw of both Output widgets, so it will take ~5 second. 1000 cell executions down the road, I have 500 output widgets, that will all take update a matplotlib figure, that is not visible, not good.

TLDR: Would be really useful to know if a widget is visible (_view_count property) so we can skip any unnecessary update.

The amount of work required to maintain `libsubmit` and `parsl` as two separate repos and packages is non-trivial. While we assumed that keeping libsubmit separately would make it easier for gateway developers and sys-admins, this cost of doing so is very high.

Pros of current split state
* libsubmit works on python3.4+, while parsl requires py3.5+
* libsubmit is substantially lightweight compared to parsl

Cons of split repos
* Added documentation, packaging and versioning effort
* Added effort for making sure parsl and libsubmit versions stay in sync
* Changes to libsubmit do not trigger parsl tests, where integration tests reside

Rather than parsing and modifying the AST directly, we can subclass a module loader which can customize how Python looks for and imports a module.

Here is an example of someone's custom module loader for reverse com domain names to link directly to Python files on the web: http://blog.dowski.com/2008/07/31/customizing-the-python-import-system/

There is a good example here of Shelve importing that can serve as a base: https://pymotw.com/3/sys/imports.html

The key is to override the `sys.meta_path` to only contain loaders that look for imports either on our DB, or in the strict paths of the Seneca `libs` folder. There needs to be consideration as to how packages from the `libs` folder import packages from the main code repository.

issue_title: How to train an agent with keras-rl?
issue_body:
Hi, I'm a beginner in RL and I'm trying to understand how to use these models and how to train agents with custom environments. 

I was trying to train an agent with the Hotter Colder environment which is included with Openai's gym environment. However, I keep getting an error that says "ValueError: Error when checking input: expected flatten_1_input to have 3 dimensions, but got array with shape (1, 1)"

If anyone can look at my code (which is below) and explain to me what I'm doing wrong, that would be great! Thank you!

import numpy as np
import gym
import gym.spaces

from keras.models import Sequential
from keras.layers import Dense, Activation, Flatten
from keras.optimizers import Adam

from rl.agents.dqn import DQNAgent
from rl.policy import BoltzmannQPolicy
from rl.memory import SequentialMemory

ENV_NAME = 'HotterColder-v0'

env = gym.make(ENV_NAME)
np.random.seed(123)
env.seed(123)
nb_actions = env.action_space.shape[0]

model = Sequential()
print(model)
model.add(Flatten(input_shape= (1,env.observation_space.n)))
model.add(Dense(16))
model.add(Activation('relu'))
model.add(Dense(nb_actions))
model.add(Activation('linear'))
print(model.summary())

memory = SequentialMemory(limit=50000, window_length=1)
policy = BoltzmannQPolicy()
dqn = DQNAgent(model=model, nb_actions=nb_actions, memory=memory, nb_steps_warmup=10,
               target_model_update=1e-2, policy=policy)
dqn.compile(Adam(lr=1e-3), metrics=['mae'])

dqn.fit(env, nb_steps=50000, visualize=False, verbose=2)

dqn.save_weights('dqn_{}_weights.h5f'.format(ENV_NAME), overwrite=True)

dqn.test(env, nb_episodes=5, visualize=True)

issue_title: rebuild_yara_rule method does not correctly quote metadata fields
issue_body:
Hey,

In this part of the rebuild yara method there is no accounting for the data type present in the value of each metadata key which means resulting rules have metadata 
```
# Rule Metadata
        if rule.get('metadata'):
            unpacked_meta = [u'\n\t\t{key} = {value}'.format(key=k, value=v)
                             for k, v in rule['metadata'].items()]
            rule_meta = u'\n\tmeta:{}\n'.format(u''.join(unpacked_meta))
        else:
            rule_meta = u''
```

A lazy fix for this would be to alter it so that we assume every value in the metadata is a string:

```
if rule.get('metadata'):
            unpacked_meta = [u'\n\t\t{key} = "{value}"'.format(key=k, value=v)
                             for k, v in rule['metadata'].items()]
            rule_meta = u'\n\tmeta:{}\n'.format(u''.join(unpacked_meta))
        else:
            rule_meta = u''
```

There might be a cleverer way of figuring it out though, since there are probably only 3 cases (int,str,list) that are ever used.

Cheers,
Tom

issue_title: pathlib2 pinning to 2.1.0 creates a version conflict with latest pytest 3.7.1
issue_body:
The new pytest version 3.7.1 requires pathlib2 >= 2.2.0.

This leads to a version conflict with requirementslib, which I use for a release helper library to parse Pipfile.lock for some checks. That library also needs pytest.

Seeing as there is already a commit unpinning pathlib2, could you please release a new version containing that commit?

Many thanks in advance

issue_title: Docs start off on the wrong foot
issue_body:
The opening sentence of the docs is poor:

> virtualenv is a successor to workingenv, and an extension of virtual-python.

No one knows what workingenv or virtual-python are.  The successorship was important when virtualenv was first released, but now virtualenv is the clear choice for these things, and people don't need this bit of historical trivia.  The Ford Mustang is no longer marketed as the "successor to the Edsel".

Instead, move the first sentence of "What It Does" to the top:\

> virtualenv is a tool to create isolated Python environments.
